data.title: March/April Readers Tagged 7 Ideal Dividend Dogs From 12 'Safer' Of 36
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a reader favorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, it is possible that only rogues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction. Examples include the reader who missed my "safer" dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios. The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha.

This month a reader asked if Qualified dividend stocks could be flagged. YCharts is taking that request up with their product development team.

My ten-year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers.

Still, every month readers have grumbled that they can’t find my ten Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

This year SA has listed all the postings on my Dogcatcher investing group by date. So to find the summary and reference guide to each dog of the week portfolio look at:

August 22, 2015 for I

October 13, 2016 for II

September 12, 2017 for III

September 13, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 12, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024 and will post its last return on October 3, 2025.

Last winter a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher.

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However I find the number of charts are a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are frequent complaints of too many words for little information. This week somebody thought I was paid by the word. No such luck.

A July 2024 prize comment was:

“anybody able to plough through this cruff and figuire which two stocks are buys in accordance with his title? these articles are borderline autistic.

additionally i do not think INTC is not overbought. it's 14-day RSI of 48.11 indicates that the company is trading in a technically neutral state. The RSI is a stock indicator that ranges from 0 to 100, with overbought stocks being above 70 and oversold stocks being below 30.

—weitermachen 8/15/24

I replied:

“@weitermachen Thanks for your comments! Just to clarify and crystallize the two suggested ideal dogs noted my July Dogs of the Dow article, are the two Dow Index stocks showing dividends (from $1k invested) exceeding single share price: Verizon Communications, and Dow Inc.

As for the "overbought" three, I'm using an unsophisticated measure (unlike RSI) to back up my assertion. "Unbuyable" stocks is perhaps a better term for Goldman, JPMorgan, & Intel, whose dividend yields exceed their free-cash-flow yields. I have since employed terms ”cash rich” or “cash poor” to describe the status of ideal and not so ideal stocks.

Regards, FA

In January I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, MoPays, and all my other articles, less obfuscating.

I now describe the “safer” stocks as “cash rich” and the stocks whose dividends from $1K in vested do not equal their single share price as “cash poor”.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

[See a summary of ten fair-priced March/April ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and seven “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, recently spun off from Liberty media. A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Currently priced at $21.16, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 5.03%! A little over $1k investment now buys 50 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 2 times the share price. Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 20 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 5/6/25 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $2,838.50)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1,000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader wdjaxOn responded to my suggestion that besides waiting for the market prices to drop or higher dividends to be declared for stocks, that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 24.58% To 78.63% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By March/April, 2025

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this March/April, 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March/April, 2026 were:

ZIM Integrated Shipping (ZIM) was projected to net $786.27, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. A beta number was not available for ZIM.

Energy Transfer (ET) was projected to net $485.37, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 77% over the market as a whole.

Best Buy (BBY) was projected to net $358.61, based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% greater than the market as a whole.

Kohl’s Corp (KSS) was projected to net $349.77, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% more than the market as a whole.

Orchid Island Capital Inc (ORC) was projected to net $346.32, based on the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% over the market as a whole.

LyondellBasell (LYB) was projected to net $316.45, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $289.54 based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% under the market as a whole.

NextEra Energy (NEE) was projected to net $264.83, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 31% under the market as a whole.

Chevron (CVX) was projected to net $261.88 based on the median of target prices estimated by 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 17% less than the market as a whole.

Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) was projected to net $245.78, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 37.05% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 1% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

The March/April, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Analyst Targets

The March/April, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top March/April ReFa/Ro Stocks By Yield Ranged 8.09% To 84.42% Per YCharts

Top ten ReFa/Ro Dogs occupied four of eleven Morningstar sectors, 4 Open Ended Investment Companies (ETFs) and also included a closed end investment corporation (CEIC).

One industrial sector member led the pack, ZIM Integrated Shipping [1]. Then four ETFs, placed second, fifth, seventh and eighth, Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC) [2], Global X NASDAQ covered Call ETF (QYLD) [5], Global X Nasdaq covered Call & Growth ETF (QYLG)[7], and Global X S&P 500 covered Call & Growth ETF (XYLG)[8].

Two financial representatives placed third and sixth, Orchid Island Capital (ORC) [3], and Blue Owl Capital [6].

One materials member placed ninth. LyondellBasell (LYB) [9], and, finally, one energy representative placed tenth, Energy Transfer [10], to complete the March/April top ten ReFa/Ro Dogs by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten March/April ReFa/Ro dogs by dividend and price as of market close 5/6/2025.

Those months none of the top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs showed a less than ideal status because all ten prices are lower than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share prices. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by all ten of the highest yield March/April ReFa/Ro Dogs.

36 ReFa/Ro Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 5/6/25

The lower chart reveals 36 ReFa/Ro Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the March/Aprl ReFa/Ro was ZIM Shipping at $1.18 per dividend dollar and the most expensive was Visa at $147.73 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Fifteen of Thirty-six ReFa/Ro Dogs Show No Dividends or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Twelve ReFa/Ro so infected are joined by three non-dividend payers: , Oxford and Capital, Orchid Island Capital, Pimco Dynamic Income Fund, Global S NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF; Blue Owl Capital, Global X Nasdaq Covered Call & Growth ETF, Global X S&P 500 Covered Call & Growth ETF, LyondellBasell Industries, Prudential, PepsiCo, NextEra Energy, Coca-Cola, BancoMarco, Ecopetrol, and VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF are the fifteen.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, executive over-reach, tariffs, and uncertainty, too.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 15.83% To 41.45% Top Ten ReFa/Ro Upsides To March/April 2026; (32) Five -0.66% to -4.79% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 46.87% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 March/April ReFa/Ro Dogs As of 5/6/25 Data

Ten-top ReFa/Ro dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs selected 5/6/25 by yield, represented four of eleven sectors plus four Open End Investment Companies (ETFs) and one Closed End Investment Corporation (CEIC).

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield ReFa/Ro Dogs (34) To Deliver 41.69% Vs. (35) 28.39% Net Gains by All Ten, Come March/April, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten ReFa/Ro Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 48.87% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fourth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, ZIM Shipping, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 78.83%.

The five lowest-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs for March/April were: Oxford Lane Capital; Orchid Island Capital; Blue Owl Capital; ZIM Shipping; Energy Transfer, with prices ranging from $4.68 to $16.19.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of March/April were: Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF; Pimco Dynamic Income Fund; Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call & Growth ETF; Global X S&P 500 Covered Call & Growth ETF; LyondellBasell, with prices ranging from $16.37 to $56.21.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the markets dies, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements will gain momentum through 2025 with the exception of very few tech stocks, look for at least half of the ten ReFa/Ro Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from trade war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.

Afterword

Buy 7 of 12 ‘Safer' March/April ReFa/Ro Dogs

May, 2025, of the 36 ReFa/Ro, 12 were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields.

The seven ”Ideal” candidates from the “safer” group showed dividend yields from $1,000 invested exceeding their single share prices.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Re/Fa/Ro Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" ReFa/Ro dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Iamgold: Tangible Upside In A Golden Environment
data.description:
Investment Thesis

IAMGOLD (NYSE:IAG) is a midstream gold miner that is undergoing a key operational transformation in 2025, driven by the start of commercial production at Côté Gold, its flagship project in Ontario. This large-scale mine, with a useful life of more than 18 years and a projected AISC below $1,100/oz, marks a structural change in the company's productive profile, allowing it to move little by little to more stable jurisdictions and higher quality assets.

In addition, the context is more than favorable: gold is trading above $2,300/oz, supported by demand from central banks, global uncertainty and expectations of a more lax monetary policy. In this scenario, in Q1 2025 IAG produced 161,000 attributable ounces (+7% YoY), with revenues of $477M (+41%), adjusted EBITDA of $204.5M (+34%) and an FCF of $139.6M, tripling the previous year.

From the valuation, it is trading well below the sector: EV/EBITDA FWD of 3.8x, P/E FWD of 12.1x, and strong projected EPS growth (+123% in 2025) and ROE close to 30%. The market has not yet fully reflected the new scale and future profitability following the start-up of Côté Gold.

While there are significant risks—such as geopolitical exposure in Burkina Faso and sensitivity to the gold price—the company has a solid liquidity position ($745M), reducing leverage, and a world-class asset in the ramp-up stage.

I believe IAG combines tangible growth, compressed multiples, and operating leverage in a bullish gold cycle, making it a very attractive opportunity within the sector's mid-cap universe. Therefore, my recommendation is a strong buy.

Overview

IAMGOLD Corporation is an intermediate gold producer with principal operations in Canada and West Africa. In 2025, it is undergoing a critical transformation phase, leveraged by the entry into production of its key asset, the Côté Gold mine in Ontario (operated jointly with Sumitomo Metal Mining). This significant-scale mine represents IAG's largest project and is expected to double its consolidated production in the coming years, with a highly competitive cost profile (AISC estimated below $1,000/oz). Initial production is already underway by early 2025, and strong cash flow expansion is anticipated starting in the second half of the year.

The international context is favorable. In 2025, the gold price has resumed an upward trend, surpassing $2,300/oz due to the combination of persistent geopolitical uncertainty (conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine), doubts about the final path of the Fed's interest rates, and a general revaluation of real assets in an environment of low confidence in fiat currencies. Gold maintains its role as a safe haven against volatility and a relevant hedge against the possible depreciation of the dollar if the Fed initiates a monetary easing cycle towards the second half of the year.

IAG has taken advantage of this tailwind with a radical improvement in its financial profile. After years of stressed balance sheets, the company managed to reduce its net leverage (net debt/EBITDA in accelerated decline) thanks to higher revenues and strict capital discipline. In addition, the sale of non-core assets allowed it to focus on its core operations and improve operational efficiency.

The gold sector in general is in a stage of consolidation. Many juniors have been left behind by lack of funding, opening up opportunities for midstream producers like IAG to expand organically or inorganically. At the same time, large producers are facing reserve replacement challenges, which could drive future mergers and acquisitions.

At the macro level, the tension between weak global growth and still contractionary monetary policies fuels the demand for gold as a hedge. Although inflation has eased in the US and Europe, the resilience of energy and food prices in emerging markets remains a pressure factor. In this framework, gold continues to act as an asset for preserving value and portfolio diversification.

IAG, with its renewed operating profile, high operating leverage and compressed valuation, is positioned as one of the most attractive players in the mid-cap segment in gold to capture the next phase of the bull cycle in the metal.

Q1 2025

In Q1 2025, IAG's results show the transition to its new operating profile, driven by the growth of Côté Gold. Attributable production was 161,000 ounces (+7% YoY) , due to the contribution of 51,000 ounces from Côté, which in Q1 2024 was just beginning its startup phase.

This new operation in Canada begins to offset the lower performance of more mature mines: Essakane fell 27% YoY to 86,000 ounces and Westwood fell 25% YoY to 24,000 ounces, mainly due to lower grades.

Financially, it generated revenues of $477 MM (+41% YoY) boosted by an average gold price of $2,731/oz, 32% higher than in Q1 2024. Adjusted EBITDA reached $204.5 MM, with an increase of 34% YoY, reflecting the company's strong operating leverage in this context of record prices.

Despite the improvement in revenue, costs also moved upward: consolidated AISC was $1,908 per ounce sold, an increase of 28%, mainly attributed to the initial costs of the Côté Gold ramp-up and lower efficiencies at Essakane and Westwood.

Even so, the company managed to generate an on-site FCF of $139.6 MM, tripling the $46 MM of 1Q 2024, and closed with a total liquidity position of $745 MM, composed of $317 MM in cash and $428 MM in available credit lines, which gives it sufficient margin to continue developing its strategy without short-term financial pressure.

Valuation

With the start of commercial production at the Côté Gold mine and a substantial improvement in revenue generation, IAG combines a very attractive valuation, compressed multiples, and an exceptional growth profile that sets it apart from its peers.

In terms of valuation, it trades at multiples well below its sector and historical averages. The P/E GAAP FWD is 12.1x compared to 16.3x for the sector, while the EV/EBITDA FWD is 3.8x, when the average for the materials sector is around 7.8x. Furthermore, its EV/EBIT FWD ratio of 5.4x is more than 55% below the sector average, suggesting that the market is not yet fully appreciating the company's jump in profitability and production scale following Côté's entry into operation.

In terms of growth, it presents extraordinary figures: EBITDA grew by 471% YoY and is expected to increase another 60% in the next 12 months. The same goes for EBIT, which climbed more than 33,000% YoY and is projected to increase more than a further 100%. In terms of EPS, estimated growth for 2025 is 123% and 39% for 2026, as Côté's revenues fully impact the results. This growth rate, although probably not sustainable in the long term, does mark the beginning of a new stage for IAG with strong revaluation potential in the short and medium term.

In profitability, the improvement is evident: the net margin rose to 50%, well above the industry average (4.5%), while the ROE shot up to almost 30% and the ROA exceeded 15%. The company achieved this even without reaching its full production regime, indicating high operational efficiency. While free cash flow generation is still negative due to recent heavy investments, this is expected to reverse in the coming quarters as CAPEX normalizes.

IAMGOLD is well positioned to capitalize on a favorable market environment. With the Fed approaching the end of the high-interest cycle and a possible depreciation of the dollar, the gold price remains firmly above $2,300/oz, a level that supports extraordinary margins for low-cost producers. Furthermore, IAG is a leveraged bet on this rally, given its expected high growth and the pending appreciation of its newly acquired assets.

The company has also strengthened its balance sheet: it reduced net debt, has $348 MM in cash, and maintains a market capitalization of $3.86 B, with a total enterprise value of $4.75 B. This implies an EV/EBITDA FWD of less than 4x, an extremely attractive ratio for a mining company with recently started production expansion.

Risks

Despite the solid strategic progress that the entry into operation of Côté Gold has meant, IAG still faces several structural and current risks to which we must pay attention.

High-risk jurisdictions and their geopolitical implications continue to be a key area of concern. While Côté Gold constrains its portfolio to Canada, the Essakane mine in Burkina Faso is still an important source of production for IAG. This mine remains novel due to the security forces’ notable decline in the Sahel region. Armed faction growth alongside internal strife has intensified political funneling. These factors pose heightened operational and logistical risks. The situation may deteriorate to a point where production is impaired, forced interruptions become necessary, or even full stoppages if conditions decline further. Regardless, the company continues to profess unreserved operational support. That said, the volatile world remains one of the most hard-to-manage factors.

As project operations, Côté Gold is classified more as an intentional endeavor, operationally a ramp-through the initial scale phase. The untapped potential of the region serves as an advantage for the company in terms of refining and optimizing procurement, but is also bound to trigger competition from other players. The projection of outputs suggests the mine commencing its commercial output only in 2024, yet achieving sustenance and optimal output sustainable production alongside maximum efficiency is still years out. Any deviation from projections would impact results and market confidence. The company estimates a stabilized AISC around $1,100/oz, but this may change due to inflationary pressures or technical problems.

Another relevant risk is associated with the sensitivity of its business model to the price of gold. IAMGOLD, as an intermediate producer with a still tight cost profile, is leveraged to the price of the metal. Although the current context is favorable, with prices above $2,300/oz, a reversal of the cycle—whether due to an aggressive strengthening of the dollar, an unexpected rise in real rates, or lower central bank appetite for gold reserves—could quickly erode margins. From a financial perspective, although the company has improved its debt profile after the sale of non-strategic assets, it continues to operate with considerable leverage for a mining company of its size. Any unexpected pressure on cash flows – whether from lower production, higher costs or falling gold – could limit its financial flexibility in an environment where external financing has become more demanding.

Lastly, climate and environmental risk should not be discounted, especially in Canadian assets, where regulation is strict and social pressure on mining is increasing. Any environmental incident at Côté Gold could result in regulatory sanctions or reputational costs that are difficult to reverse.

Conclusions

After analyzing IAMGOLD, I consider that it offers one of the most attractive recovery and expansion theses within the universe of medium-sized gold miners.

Its renewed production profile, coupled with Côté Gold's transformative potential, financial discipline and highly underrated valuation position it as a leveraged but controlled play in a structurally strong gold environment.

IAG has wide margins, tangible growth and ongoing operational catalysts, so I recommend a strong buy for investors with a medium risk tolerance and a 12 to 24 month view.

data.title: Recession Risk Ruled Out? That Makes VGLT More Attractive.
data.description:
When I mention VGLT as an opportunity in conversations with fellow analysts and advisors in my research work for banks and financial networks, the response I hear more and more often is:

“Right now? But don’t you see what’s happening?”

And understandably so, after a –0.3% drop in U.S. GDP growth, an alarm bell went off in the minds of many investors, ringing “recession - recession - recession”.

However, moving from theory to practice, I believe this fear might actually start to be interpreted as a contrarian indicator. And now I’ll explain why.

But first, why VGLT and not TLT?

VGLT holds 90 U.S. government bonds with an average yield to maturity of 4.6%, an average effective maturity of 22.2 years, and an average duration of 14.5 years. It also features an incredibly low expense ratio of 0.03%, lowered by Vanguard itself in February.

This is a key advantage for VGLT, even when compared to the top competitor in this category: TLT.

VGLT was already very competitive in this regard, but the February fee cut has made the cost gap much more significant, an element that, over the long term, will work in VGLT’s favor, especially when it comes to performance.

How the Bond Market Fooled Everyone

I like to remind myself every day that, to make a difference as an investor, I need to seek the truth where no one wants to look, and not just follow the narrative imposed by the media stream. I believe that’s the key to being a good analyst, or at least, I’ve promised myself to make it mine.

And in the bond market, in my opinion, many got trapped last year by the rate cut narrative. “Hey, rates will fall, so buy TLT!” or “Long durations are the most sensitive to rate cuts, they’re coming for sure.”

But things turned out differently, and the yield curve proves it:

Exactly one year ago, short-term maturities offered higher yields, peaking at 5.38%, while 20+ year maturities were at 4.735%. Those who positioned themselves in long-term bonds, expecting the Fed to follow the ECB in cutting rates, found themselves, one year later, with a total return just above 1%, comforting themselves with the idea that the net yield to maturity will eventually be higher.

Meanwhile, solutions like BIL ETF delivered 4.80%.

Considering core PCE inflation at 2.6% from the latest reading, the first case produced a negative real return, while the second was positive.

And more: after the April sell-offs, the total return of ultra-short and short-term ETFs like BIL even outperformed the S&P 500.

That, too, seemed absurd to many, but then again, if the ERP is negative, there’s a reason.

Why Long-Term Bonds

Back to the point, today, the curve is completing its steepening phase. Let me be clear: I’m not saying that ultra-short or short-term solutions have become worthless, on the contrary, I still find them interesting in this market.

What I am saying, however, is that in my view, long-term options like VGLT have become attractive again, and the reason is precisely: “recession.”

Here’s my macro view:

  • PCE inflation is still high, but steadily declining, currently at 2.6%

  • The unemployment rate is stable at low levels, around 4.2%

  • U.S. GDP for Q1 2025 contracted by 0.3%

  • Imports increased by 41.3%

How do I interpret this? The economy remains resilient, and the GDP contraction is artificial, driven by excessive imports, which are treated as a negative component in GDP calculations.

And it’s a view that was partially shared by Powell and policymakers during Wednesday’s FOMC meeting, where they decided to keep Fed rates in the 4.25%–4.5% range.

Here’s my technical view

The market is currently pricing in, with over a 30% probability, Fed funds rates in the 350–375 bps range by year-end (based on the CME FedWatch Tool). S&P Global forecasts U.S. GDP growth at a 1.9%, and the GDPNow model doesn’t project a negative Q2, on the contrary, it currently estimates +1.1%.

Today’s interest rates are not excessive for the economy, but the market expects cuts by 2025, with the recession narrative being politically induced, mainly by Trump’s threats, which seem to aim directly at lowering rates (and, I’d add, the dollar).

It’s also worth noting that VGLT holdings declined in price right after the tariff announcements, in line with the deep recession narrative. This is precisely why I see VGLT becoming increasingly bullish.

Following this logic, the fact that the Fed is keeping rates steady is currently a positive sign, it means the economy is balanced at these levels. And the market expecting rate cuts in 2025? I see it as a natural progression if we remove the media noise.

Risk

What concerns me most is the idea that this process could drag on for too long, and not align with the upcoming 2025–2026 maturities (I discussed this in detail here).

This scenario, not driven by an autoinduced recession, is one that many analysts are currently debating. In this case, the recession would be more structural, and could have a permanent impact, even in the event of rate cuts.

Here, volatility in long durations would increase, along with the market’s perceived risk, and that’s something that can’t be ignored.

Conclusion

But speculation aside, the current situation seems more under control than how it’s often portrayed. Why? Because if, due to tariffs, the U.S. economy were to enter a “controlled” recession, long-term bonds may have already priced in that scenario.

The real risk lies in rates staying high for too long, as debt could then weigh more heavily, and more durably, on corporate balance sheets. In that case, volatility on long maturities could rise.

That said, paradoxically, I believe it’s precisely in moments like these that ETFs like VGLT strike the most favorable risk-reward balance.

data.title: IBM: Fundamentals Don't Back Up The Bull Run
data.description:
Here is a nice example of yet another stock where fundamentals and price action seem mostly out of sync. I am talking about no other than the 100-year-old tech giant, International Business Machines Corporation (IBM).

Take a look at the daily chart below, showing how IBM's stock has performed over the past decade.

You did notice the +100% return since mid-2023, right? If you're anything like me, you're probably thinking that revenue must have grown by double digits every quarter since then. Am I right? Well, take a look at the chart below, showing yoy revenue growth for each quarter since Q2 2022.

O oh.

That doesn't look promising to me. I see low single-digit revenue growth. In fact, I can almost call it flat now, considering the 0.5% increase in Q1 2025.

Ok, ok. I am about profits, baby! (some may say).

Fine, take a look at the chart below, showing the big three profitability ratios.

Once again, o oh.

Gross, operating, and net income margins have been pretty much flat since mid-2023. The company hasn't improved the quality of its revenue (i.e. the margins) since then, despite the software segment (which has a gross margin of 83.6%) leading in terms of revenue contribution.

Ok. What else do we have... cash flows.

Perfect. Take a look at the yoy change in FCF and OCF since 2022.

Again, not particularly attractive considering the 100% increase in IBM's share price since mid-2023. In fact, both metrics are down over the last two years.

Anything else to sustain such a bull run? How about a hike in dividend payouts? Nope.

How about an increase in dividend yield? (some optimists still may ask)

Well, this question is answered by itself. Considering the 100% increase in IBM's share price over the past two years and the flat payout per share trend shown above, I am not surprised to see the yield coming down from ~5% in 2023 to almost less than half now.

Why do I keep using this reference; mid-2023? Has something relevant happened back then, anyway?

Nothing much... aside from the start of the AI boom.

That line of thinking is how I first looked at this stock. I know it sounds overly simple, but that's how I usually approach stocks I'm not familiar with.

So far, it seems that I am quite pessimistic about IBM, right? I am not. Would I ever bet against this trend, even if it may not be backed up by fundamentals? Let me be clear: not in a million years. Here is why.

To me, IBM for the past two years is a great example of a robust company whose stock rose with the tide. The company is very well exposed to AI. In its software segment, the watsonx platform has been the main driver for the segment's 9% yoy revenue growth. Even though this platform is not even close to Palantir's AIP, it's enough to give IBM enough exposure to AI growth, which is something that the market highly favors today.

Additionally, in the past quarter, Red Hat and automation (two units within the software segment) have been up in the double digits.

Furthermore, the company is capitalizing on the boom in generative AI in its consulting business. According to the earnings call, about 80% of IBM's $6 billion generative AI bookings are for the consulting business. Also, in the past quarter, bookings went up by $1 billion sequentially, which is a good sign of growth. That said, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne just yet. Why?

Take a look at the chart below showing margins for the two segments. Which one looks more attractive?

Unfortunately, most of the gen AI bookings are directed to the less profitable segment.

On the risks side, analysts were quite curious during the latest Q&A to find out the impact of DOGE on IBM's consulting business. Here is a quick recap.

IBM's US federal business accounts for less than 5% of the total annual revenue, with approximately 60% of that amount being consulting services. In Q1 2025, IBM disclosed that 15 U.S. federal contracts were canceled due to DOGE, totaling $100 million in future payments.

Is this a lot? Well, when you compare it with the company's annualized consulting backlog of $30 billion, this headwind is less than 1%.

And on our annualized backlog of over $30 billion in total Consulting, this is like less than $100 million of backlog over a duration of multiple years. - James Kavanaugh

Final Thoughts

To wrap up, I don't see IBM's fundamentals backing up the 100% increase in its share price over the past two years. While these fundamentals are robust, they are far from justifying a bull run of this magnitude. That said, I wouldn't short this stock as momentum has picked up, and betting against it usually ends up quite badly (speaking from my previous experience as a contrarian deep value investor).

From a valuation perspective, when compared to some of its peers, the company is not particularly cheap, especially on a P/E basis. That said, IBM looks attractive on a P/FCF basis due to its capital-light structure (compared to other peers who are investing in data center infrastructure).

Overall, I rate IBM as a hold, as I simply don't see a compelling reason for its share price to go up, other than rising with the tide.

data.title: BMAX: An Alternative To (Micro)Strategy With Less Risk
data.description:
Introduction

I've been interested in cryptocurrencies for the better part of ten years, but most of my attention has been on the coins themselves, like Bitcoin (BTC-USD), and not the corporations that operate in the space.

Of course, over that time, "Bitcoin Development Company" Strategy (MSTR), formerly MicroStrategy, had caught my attention. The core premise of the company's Bitcoin operations is that it has historically been profitable to buy Bitcoin on credit. The firm pioneered a strategy where they issue convertible debt to buy Bitcoin for their corporate treasury.

Enter the REX Bitcoin Corporate Treasury Convertible Bond ETF (BMAX), from the same advisors who run the famous FEPI ETF, which promises to invest in the convertible bonds Strategy and other firms issue to buy Bitcoin with. It launched in March 2025, carries a fee of 0.85% p.a., and invests directly in the convertible bonds of firms like MSTR as well as Riot Platforms (RIOT) and MARA Holdings (MARA).

In REX Shares' words, from their website:

The game has changed. Corporate treasuries are evolving, and public companies are now using convertible bonds to stack Bitcoin on their balance sheets. Once reserved for Wall Street’s elite, these are now available to all via ETFs. BMAX strategically focuses on entities like Strategy, a prominent issuer of Bitcoin treasury convertible bonds. This focus allows the fund to leverage the burgeoning market dynamics where digital asset strategies intersect with traditional finance.

The strategy seems fairly straightforward when illustrated by REX Shares:

Convertible Debt

The basic premise behind convertible debt is that it doesn't have to be paid in cash, but can be converted into equity to pay creditors instead. This keeps costs low for the business issuing the debt, which allows them to buy more Bitcoin.

These come with their own risks, and act as a weird in-between asset for equity and debt. These bonds move alongside the equities that issue them at times, mostly because the payoff for these instruments is more equity and not cash. The way it is being sold by REX Shares is that BMAX is less risky than MSTR because of this relationship between the equity and its convertible bonds.

We can see this relationship very clearly when looking at the price return correlation of BMAX against MSTR over the last few months, where the rolling metric is very high, but not perfect. We see the break, where they started to decouple, occur during the most volatile period in this chart, early April.

Let's take a look at the performance outright to see if it's worth holding over MSTR or Bitcoin outright. After all, the only case to be made for BMAX so far is that it is a toned down version of MSTR, but that could be achieved with a lower allocation to MSTR.

Performance & Alternatives

Performance is very limited, as BMAX is so new, having launched in March this year. However, we did get to see a large drop and recovery through this time, as the fund was launched just before the market's steep fall on April 4th.

BMAX fashions itself as less volatile and risky than holding MSTR stock, which itself is like a leveraged position on Bitcoin. Financing Bitcoin leverage for MSTR et al. doesn't right away sound like a risk-off strategy to me, especially compared to owning Bitcoin or a Bitcoin covered call fund like YBTC.

According to REX Shares:

Convertible bonds combine the security of fixed income with the potential for stock appreciation to stocks active in Bitcoin, seeking a less volatile, more balanced approach to risk and growth.

So, let's look at how the fund has held up against its competitors over the course of its short lifespan. Thankfully, we get a wider picture in this performance check than most funds younger than a quarter, thanks to recent market volatility.

While its end performance is very close to that of Bitcoin, we do see a much further drawdown than the largest Bitcoin ETF, IBIT.

Fun fact: IBIT owns nearly 3% of BTC's total supply.

True to management's claims, MSTR fared worse in the drawdown by the time we found the bottom. In a run-up, like we're seeing in the chart above, we should expect MSTR to have the highest return of all of these funds as it is the most aggressive, followed by Bitcoin outright, then convertible debt used to fund Bitcoin, and finally a Bitcoin covered call fund that sells its upside for income.

Consequently, when looking at just the drawdowns, we see the reserve order, with the most aggressive asset, MSTR, being off its highs for longer and more steeply than

BMAX Holdings

So let's take a look at what's actually in the fund, other than the general "convertible bonds" label. BMAX currently has 11 holdings, ten of them being bonds, and a cash position.

While the majority of the holdings are from MSTR, we do see a near-11% allocation to MARA bonds, and a 3.5% allocation to a RIOT bond.

Note that the fund doesn't exclude ownership of the equity they may gain from bonds being converted, but limits their holdings to 20% of NAV. That being said, the current allocation is 0% to equity outright. This is most likely because none of the holdings have been converted yet, and so BMAX hasn't had the chance to

I imagine this provision is in the fund so that the managers can choose when to sell the equity, and are not forced by the fund's rules to sell as soon as they are in possession of the shares, which could be disadvantageous based on the given market conditions when the conversions occur.

Also note that most of these bonds do not pay very high yields, or any yield at all. This is due to the convertible nature, where investors understand the upside is in the equity being acquired at a discount and not in regular payments of cash. This system is very advantageous for MSTR et al. because they are able to raise capital now to buy Bitcoin at today's prices instead of tomorrow's prices.

We can see this in the following chart, where REX Shares breaks down BMAX's holdings and highlights the price these firms purchased Bitcoin at using that round's funding. They range from the highlighted prices, under $70k, to as high as $96k.

BMAX Dividends & Yield

The fund has never paid a dividend, but it likely will at some point. This is due to the fact that there is some yield to be had in these bonds, with the highest coupons coming in at 2.25% and the lowest at 0%.

Expect the yield on the fund to be very low, likely sub 1% when all is said and done. Most of what the yield will do is help offset the 0.85% p.a. management fee.

Regarding this, REX Shares writes:

...BMAX seeks to pay quarterly dividends from interest income and bond gains. However, they are expected to be minimal as most of the convertible bonds issued by the bond issuers currently pay zero or near zero interest rates.

Suitability

Who is this fund for?

The only answer I can come up with is: people who want to invest in MSTR, but are afraid of volatility.

That may sound like an oxymoron to some, but there must be a market out there for these folks, given BMAX exists, and the bonds do sell on the market.

I'm not going to give allocation guidance for BMAX in particular, because I don't see a world in which it's more suitable for investors than owning a Bitcoin ETF, like IBIT.

This fund is definitely not suitable for moderate and conservative investors, but neither are MSTR and Bitcoin. The aggressive investors already interested in the asset are likely better off owning MSTR outright, and taking advantage of the higher risk, higher reward environment that being on the issuance side of the convertible debt offers. Investors that believe MSTR is too risky are better off owning Bitcoin through an ETF like IBIT than BMAX, as they won't get the downside that comes with MSTR bonds; that downside appears to be more than IBIT's own, based on BMAX's short history.

The risk and reward may not be right for most folks.

As a counter-weight, here is REX Shares' answer to the "who should consider this fund" question:

BMAX may be a good fit for investors who want to tap into Bitcoin’s long-term potential—but with a more balanced, risk-aware approach. It’s designed for people who:

  • Want exposure to companies investing in Bitcoin, without holding crypto directly
  • Prefer less day-to-day volatility than owning Bitcoin or mining stocks
  • Like the structure of a regulated ETF that trades in a regular brokerage account
  • Are looking for a way to diversify their portfolio with digital asset themes, but still want built-in protection

If you’re curious about Bitcoin but want a smarter, more strategic way to participate, BMAX could be a good starting point

They may seem to contradict me, as "less day-to-day volatility than owning Bitcoin" hasn't proven true so far, but it may just be too early to tell for sure. I want to give them the benefit of the doubt on that one.

It should also be noted that "Like the structure of a regulated ETF that trades in a regular brokerage account" does also apply to IBIT. While I use it for a direct comparison to BMAX, since it is an ETF that owns Bitcoin, the fund managers are referencing BMAX as an alternative to self-custody or exchange-traded Bitcoin, like on Coinbase (COIN), Robinhood (HOOD), or eToro (ETOR), where investors can buy paper Bitcoin held by the exchange to trade spot prices, as opposed to an ETF.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I struggle to find an answer to "why would I invest in the REX Bitcoin Corporate Treasury Convertible Bond ETF (BMAX)?" It doesn't seem to offer the same kind of protection from a tanking Bitcoin market that it claims, and over its short lifetime, has shown worse resilience than Bitcoin ETFs. However, the fund is definitely a risk-off version of Strategy, and does provide investors access to assets not usually available to retail investors, which does earn BMAX some points.

While I struggle to recommend it, the fund is serving its purpose as a way for retail investors to get access to the convertible debt strategy MSTR pioneered in the corporate Bitcoin space.

This fund needs a lot more time to be properly reviewed, likely years, because how it reacts to when its bonds convert or mature will be telling of long-term performance. The fund is too new for these events to happen yet, and so it's unclear how the fund will react currently. Its first bond doesn't mature until 2028, and has a put date in 2027. We'll have to wait for that to play out before we can really evaluate the fund's performance against alternatives.

Thanks for reading.
